items.q: What is the difference between a theme and a symbol?
items.correct: A theme is an abstract idea or concern the text explores (e.g. mortality); a symbol is a concrete image that stands for something else (e.g. a skull).
items.misconception: Themes are just symbols repeated through the book.
items.random_wrong: A theme is the title of the book.
items.why: Students treat theme and symbol as the same thing. Themes are ideas, symbols are images. Skulls (symbol) can develop the theme (mortality), but they are not the same level of abstraction.

items.q: What is the structural framework of The Canterbury Tales?
items.correct: A frame narrative — pilgrims travelling to Canterbury tell stories along the way, allowing Chaucer to juxtapose different voices, genres and social classes.
items.misconception: It is one continuous story told by Chaucer from beginning to end.
items.random_wrong: It is a religious text used in Catholic services.
items.why: Students miss the frame structure. Chaucer's strategy is polyphony — voicing many speakers to compare and contrast — not a single narrative voice.

items.q: What is the difference between a metaphor and a simile?
items.correct: A simile compares using 'like' or 'as'; a metaphor states one thing IS another, making an implicit comparison without those connectives.
items.misconception: Metaphors are short and similes are long.
items.random_wrong: Metaphors are true and similes are false.
items.why: Length is irrelevant. The defining difference is the use of 'like'/'as' in similes — metaphors omit them, making the comparison stronger and more direct.

items.q: Beginning a narrative 'in the middle of the action' is called:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: In medias res. “Ab ovo” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: A nested frame narrative. “Single first-person narration” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Great Gatsby was published in:
items.why: The real figure is 1925. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
